comorbidities.name: Acute health anxiety with panic attacks

pathophysiology_summary: Melasma is an acquired disorder of hypermelanosis driven by melanocyte hyperactivity in genetically predisposed individuals, with three principal triggers: ultraviolet (and visible-light) radiation, female sex hormones (pregnancy, combined oral contraceptives), and genetic predisposition. This patient has the classic centrofacial pattern — symmetrical light-to-dark brown macules and patches over the malar cheeks, central forehead and cutaneous upper lip — that developed gradually over months and then darkened abruptly after intense UV exposure over a beach weekend, on a background of a combined oral contraceptive pill commenced three months earlier. The pigmentation is purely macular (no induration, scaling, atrophy or ulceration) and strictly symmetrical, which distinguishes it from a solitary evolving pigmented neoplasm (lentigo maligna / melanoma). Systemic hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases and oral mucosa is absent and the patient is systemically well, arguing strongly against primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). The alarming tempo of onset is entirely explained by UV-driven melanogenesis, not malignancy. The acute presentation is dominated by a benign cosmetic diagnosis complicated by severe, disproportionate health anxiety.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical brown facial pigmentation in a young woman on the pill with recent heavy sun exposure — even before detailed history the pattern points to melasma.

The patient's own fear; any facial pigmentation warrants a deliberate check for a solitary asymmetric evolving lesion before reassurance — but the symmetrical bilateral macular pattern argues against it.
Raised by the triage note; hyperpigmentation can be adrenal, so a systemic screen (palmar creases, mucosa, constitutional symptoms) must be done — expected negative here.
Plausible for facial pigmentation but requires a preceding inflammatory dermatosis in the same distribution, which is absent.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should keep melasma leading while explicitly listing melanoma and Addison's as important-not-to-miss items to be actively excluded — not accept the patient's 'skin cancer' self-diagnosis, and not reflexively escalate on it either.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetry, purely macular (no texture change/scaling/bleeding), sun-exposed distribution, OCP + acute UV trigger — the full melasma constellation.
Downgraded but never fully excludable remotely: symmetrical bilateral macules without a single evolving/asymmetric/bleeding lesion make melanoma very unlikely; dermoscopy cannot be done over video.
Screened negative — no constitutional symptoms, normal palmar creases and oral mucosa, pigmentation confined to sun-exposed face.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the pertinent negatives (DI-004) and adrenal screen (DI-005), Addison's is excluded and melanoma is downgraded to a low-probability watch item; melasma is confidently leading.

differential.evidence_basis: Benign hormonally- and UV-driven facial hypermelanosis; clinical diagnosis achievable on history + video appearance.
Retained only as the safety-net item: any future change to a single lesion (growth, asymmetry, bleeding, crusting) warrants a routine-to-urgent in-person skin check with dermoscopy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct final position is confident benign melasma with reassurance, sun protection and routine GP review — NOT urgent biopsy/ED, and NOT dismissive discharge without a skin-check safety-net.

red_flags.description: A single pigmented lesion that is asymmetric, has an irregular growing border, colour variegation, is enlarging, bleeding, crusting or ulcerating (melanoma features)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would shift concern to melanoma/lentigo maligna and mandate urgent in-person dermoscopy/biopsy; its ABSENCE (symmetrical, uniform, purely macular pigmentation) is what makes benign melasma the confident diagnosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If such a lesion were present and dismissed as melasma, a melanoma could be missed.

red_flags.description: Systemic hyperpigmentation of palmar creases/oral mucosa with constitutional features (fatigue, weight loss, postural dizziness, nausea/vomiting, hypotension) — primary adrenal insufficiency
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise Addison's disease and, if the patient were unwell/hypotensive, an adrenal crisis requiring emergency care; screened negative here (well, normal mucosa/palms, pigmentation confined to sun-exposed face).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A genuine Addisonian crisis mistaken for a cosmetic skin problem could deteriorate to shock.

red_flags.description: Severe psychological distress / panic with functional impairment secondary to the health anxiety
red_flags.clinical_significance: Not a physical red flag but the acute driver of the presentation — must be addressed with reassurance and de-escalation; unmanaged panic could prompt an unnecessary ED attendance.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient's terror and vivid 'spreading black skin cancer' framing can push the AI Doctor to over-investigate or over-escalate a benign, classic melasma pattern.
Accepting the patient's 'skin cancer' label rather than independently reading the symmetrical, macular, sun-distributed pattern that defines melasma.
Treating the 48-hour darkening as evidence of malignant speed, when abrupt UV-driven darkening of pre-existing melasma is expected and benign.
Over-correcting to pure reassurance without giving a skin-check safety-net for any future single changing lesion, or without addressing the OCP trigger.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Dermoscopic assessment of any individual lesion — cannot be performed over video
Definitive exclusion of melanoma in an atypical or solitary lesion (requires in-person dermoscopy ± biopsy)
Serum morning cortisol / short Synacthen test and electrolytes to formally exclude adrenal insufficiency (not required here given a negative clinical screen)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation. Provide clinical reassurance that the appearance is a benign, symmetrical, hormonally- and UV-driven pigmentation with no features of malignancy, start sun protection, and arrange routine GP review (2–4 weeks) to discuss the contraceptive pill and topical lightening options; advise a prompt in-person skin check only if a single lesion later changes.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-directional: (1) falsely reassuring a genuinely atypical/asymmetric evolving lesion as melasma could miss melanoma; (2) over-triaging classic symmetrical melasma to urgent biopsy/ED causes unnecessary anxiety, cost and resource use. The video-visible symmetrical macular pattern plus a negative lesion and adrenal screen supports confident benign management with a targeted safety-net.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Melasma is a benign acquired hypermelanosis; broad-spectrum SPF50+ sunscreen and photoprotection are the cornerstone of management, with removal of hormonal triggers (e.g. OCP) and topical depigmenting agents (e.g. hydroquinone) as options.

First-line management of melasma is rigorous sun protection and trigger avoidance; hydroquinone-based topical therapy may be used for cosmetically troublesome pigmentation; skin irritation (harsh scrubs/peels) can worsen pigmentation.
